fast.summary: IHM (Item History Model) tackles cold-start recommendations by injecting user-interaction signals directly into the item tower of a Two Tower model, combined with attention-based pooling for inductive inference without retraining. This enables real-time, high-relevance recommendations for newly published content at scale.
fast.key_idea: Inject user interaction history into the item embedding tower and use attention-based pooling to generalize inductively to new items without requiring model retraining.
fast.why_it_matters: Cold-start latency and relevance are critical bottlenecks in production recommendation systems; IHM eliminates the retraining cycle while improving ranking quality for new content, directly impacting content creator growth and user engagement.
possible_use_cases: Real-time recommendations for newly uploaded videos or articles on content platforms
Cold-start product recommendations for newly listed e-commerce items
Rapid surfacing of new social media posts to relevant audiences without waiting for model retraining

deep.method_breakdown: IHM augments the item tower of a Two Tower retrieval model by directly injecting user-interaction signals (e.g., historical user engagements with the cold start item) into the item representation, rather than relying solely on item content features. It uses attention-based pooling over these interaction signals to produce a fixed-size item embedding inductively, meaning the item embedding can be computed on-the-fly at inference time without retraining. This inductive design allows newly uploaded content to immediately receive a high-quality embedding as soon as even a few user interactions are observed.
deep.key_innovation: Prior cold start work either relies on content-only features (losing collaborative signal) or requires periodic model retraining to incorporate new interaction data (causing latency). IHM's novelty is combining both: it injects interaction data directly into the item tower at inference time via attention-based pooling, making the model inductive rather than transductive. This eliminates the retraining bottleneck while still capturing user-item interaction signals that standard Two Tower models can only approximate through inner-product similarity.
deep.technical_insight: The critical design choice is attention-based pooling over user-interaction histories injected into the item tower. This works because attention allows the model to selectively weight which user interactions are most informative for representing the item (e.g., high-engagement users vs. accidental clicks), producing a robust item embedding even from sparse early interactions. Injecting this into the item tower — rather than the user tower or a fusion layer — preserves the Two Tower retrieval structure needed for ANN search while dramatically increasing the expressiveness of the item embedding for cold start scenarios.
deep.limitations: IHM requires at least some user interactions to be useful; items with zero interactions still fall back to content-only features, so the very earliest cold start window is not addressed. The attention pooling over interaction histories adds inference-time compute that may not scale easily to extremely high-traffic platforms with millions of new items per hour. The approach also inherits Two Tower limitations for long-tail users. Additionally, the paper does not deeply analyze how the quality degrades as a function of the number of initial interactions, making it hard to tune deployment thresholds.
interview_questions: Why does the standard Two Tower model suffer from information loss, and how does IHM specifically address this for cold start items?
What makes IHM 'inductive' and why is that property essential for real-time cold start recommendations?
How does attention-based pooling over user interactions differ from simply averaging interaction embeddings, and what benefit does it provide?
What are the trade-offs of injecting user-interaction signals into the item tower versus a cross-attention fusion layer between the two towers?
How does IHM maintain compatibility with approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search at serving time despite the richer item embeddings?
interview_answers: Two Tower models encode users and items independently and capture relevance only through inner-product similarity, meaning fine-grained feature interactions are limited. For cold start items, training data sparsity means the item tower has almost no collaborative signal to learn from, so the item embedding is driven entirely by content features and is poorly calibrated to actual user preferences. IHM addresses this by directly feeding observed user-interaction signals into the item tower at inference time, allowing the embedding to reflect real engagement patterns even before sufficient training data accumulates.

A model is inductive if it can generalize to unseen entities without retraining — it computes embeddings from observed features and interactions via a fixed function. IHM achieves this through attention-based pooling: given any set of user interactions for a new item, the model can immediately compute a high-quality item embedding using the learned pooling function. This is essential because retraining (transductive approaches) introduces hours of latency before new content can be served, which is unacceptable when users are actively generating content that needs to reach audiences quickly.

Simple averaging treats all interactions equally, which is noisy — accidental views or low-quality signals dilute the embedding. Attention-based pooling learns to assign higher weights to interactions that are more informative (e.g., long watches, saves, shares) and can condition the weights on the item's own content features, producing a more semantically precise aggregation. This is particularly important for cold start where each interaction carries high marginal information value.

Injecting into the item tower preserves the decoupled structure of Two Tower models, so the item embedding is a standalone vector usable for pre-computation and ANN indexing. A cross-attention fusion layer would couple user and item representations at query time, requiring a full forward pass per user-item pair — this is computationally infeasible for retrieval over millions of candidates. The item-tower injection trades some modeling expressiveness for the scalability required in production retrieval.

Because IHM still produces a single fixed-size item embedding vector (via the attention pooling), it is compatible with standard ANN indexes (e.g., HNSW, FAISS). The key is that the item embedding is computed once when the item is ingested or when early interactions arrive, stored in the ANN index, and then retrieved via inner product with the user tower output — the same serving pipeline as a standard Two Tower model, just with richer item vectors.
